agent_output: {"executive_summary":"A food delivery platform connecting restaurants, customers, and delivery drivers — designed as a modular system with six bounded contexts. The architecture uses event-driven communication between contexts to keep coupling low, a central Order Lifecycle context as the orchestrating core domain, and clear anti-corruption layers between the three actor types (restaurant, customer, driver). The design prioritizes independent evolvability of each context, real-time location/status tracking, and transactional integrity around payments and order state transitions.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Customer Gateway","responsibility":"Customer-facing API: browsing restaurants/menus, placing orders, tracking delivery status, managing addresses/payment methods, order history.","boundaries":"Owns customer profile, saved addresses, favorites. Does NOT own menu data or order state — reads from Restaurant Catalog and Order Lifecycle via events/queries."},{"name":"Restaurant Catalog & Operations","responsibility":"Menu management, restaurant availability/hours, item pricing, prep-time estimates. Receives incoming orders and manages restaurant-side acceptance/rejection and prep-status updates.","boundaries":"Owns menu items, categories, pricing, restaurant profiles, operating hours. Publishes MenuUpdated, RestaurantOnline/Offline events. Does not own order state after acceptance."},{"name":"Order Lifecycle","responsibility":"The orchestrating core: manages the full order state machine from placement through delivery confirmation. Coordinates between customer, restaurant, and driver contexts. Enforces business rules (cancellation windows, refund eligibility, order validation).","boundaries":"Owns Order aggregate (items snapshot, state, timestamps, actor references). Publishes OrderPlaced, OrderAccepted, OrderReadyForPickup, OrderPickedUp, OrderDelivered, OrderCancelled events."},{"name":"Delivery & Logistics","responsibility":"Driver onboarding, real-time location tracking, driver-order matching/dispatch, route optimization, ETA calculation, driver availability management.","boundaries":"Owns Driver aggregate (profile, vehicle, location, availability status, active delivery). Consumes OrderReadyForPickup. Publishes DriverAssigned, DriverArrivedAtRestaurant, DriverArrivedAtCustomer, DeliveryCompleted."},{"name":"Payments & Billing","responsibility":"Payment processing, order total calculation (subtotal + fees + tax + tip), restaurant payouts, driver earnings, refunds, promo/coupon redemption.","boundaries":"Owns PaymentIntent, Transaction, Payout, PromoCode aggregates. Integrates with external payment gateway (Stripe/similar). Publishes PaymentAuthorized, PaymentCaptured, RefundIssued."},{"name":"Notifications","responsibility":"Multi-channel notifications (push, SMS, email) for all actors. Templating, delivery preferences, rate limiting.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates and delivery preferences. Stateless consumer of domain events — never owns business state. Generic subdomain."},{"name":"Identity & Access","responsibility":"Authentication, authorization, role management (customer, restaurant admin, driver, platform admin). Session management, OAuth/SSO.","boundaries":"Owns User, Role, Session. Generic subdomain — use off-the-shelf (Auth0, Cognito, or similar) where possible."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Restaurant Catalog & Operations","contract":"Sync query: GET /restaurants, GET /restaurants/{id}/menu — read-only. Customer Gateway holds a read-optimized projection of nearby restaurants and menus, updated via MenuUpdated events.","coupling_type":"loose — event-sourced read model"},{"from":"Customer Gateway","to":"Order Lifecycle","contract":"Sync command: POST /orders (place order), GET /orders/{id} (status). Order Lifecycle validates and returns order confirmation.","coupling_type":"moderate — synchronous command for order placement, async events for status updates"},{"from":"Order Lifecycle","to":"Restaurant Catalog & Operations","contract":"Async event: OrderPlaced → restaurant receives incoming order. Restaurant publishes OrderAccepted/OrderRejected/OrderReadyForPickup back as events.","coupling_type":"loose — event-driven"},{"from":"Order Lifecycle","to":"Delivery & Logistics","contract":"Async event: OrderAccepted triggers pre-dispatch matching. OrderReadyForPickup triggers final driver dispatch. Delivery context publishes DriverAssigned, DeliveryCompleted.","coupling_type":"loose — event-driven"},{"from":"Order Lifecycle","to":"Payments & Billing","contract":"Sync command for payment authorization at order placement. Async event OrderDelivered triggers payment capture. OrderCancelled triggers refund flow.","coupling_type":"moderate — sync for auth, async for capture/refund"},{"from":"Notifications","to":"All Contexts","contract":"Subscribes to domain events (OrderPlaced, DriverAssigned, OrderDelivered, etc.) and dispatches appropriate notifications to the relevant actor.","coupling_type":"loose — pure event consumer"},{"from":"All Contexts","to":"Identity & Access","contract":"Token validation on every request. Thin middleware/sidecar pattern — no domain coupling.","coupling_type":"loose — infrastructure concern"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer browses restaurants (Customer Gateway → read projection from Restaurant Catalog). 2. Customer places order (Customer Gateway → Order Lifecycle → Payments for auth → Restaurant Catalog for acceptance). 3. Restaurant accepts and preps (Restaurant Catalog → OrderAccepted event → Order Lifecycle). 4. Driver dispatched (Order Lifecycle → OrderAccepted → Delivery & Logistics pre-matches; OrderReadyForPickup → final dispatch). 5. Driver picks up and delivers (Delivery & Logistics → location stream → Order Lifecycle/Customer Gateway for tracking). 6. Delivery confirmed (Delivery & Logistics → DeliveryCompleted → Order Lifecycle → Payments captures payment → Notifications sends receipts). All status transitions emit domain events consumed by Notifications for real-time updates to all three actors."},"patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture","rationale":"The three actor types (customer, restaurant, driver) operate on different timelines and have different interaction patterns. Events decouple these naturally and enable real-time status propagation without polling."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Read-heavy operations (browsing menus, tracking orders) vastly outnumber writes. Separate read-optimized projections for restaurant search/menu browsing and order tracking allow independent scaling and optimization."},{"pattern":"Saga / Process Manager","rationale":"Order lifecycle spans multiple services (payment auth → restaurant acceptance → driver dispatch → delivery → payment capture). A saga in the Order Lifecycle context orchestrates compensating actions on failure (e.g., refund if restaurant rejects)."},{"pattern":"State Machine for Order Lifecycle","rationale":"Orders have well-defined states and transitions with business rules at each gate. A state machine makes invalid transitions impossible and the lifecycle auditable."},{"pattern":"Anti-Corruption Layer","rationale":"External payment gateway and potential third-party restaurant POS integrations need ACLs to prevent external models from leaking into our domain."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (future)","rationale":"Start as a modular monolith with clear context boundaries. Extract to microservices only when scaling demands it — the event-driven boundaries make this extraction mechanical, not architectural."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Lifecycle","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId. Entities: OrderLineItem. VOs: DeliveryAddress, OrderTotal, OrderStatus. Invariants: valid state transitions, cancellation window enforcement, minimum order amount)","OrderSaga (orchestrates cross-context coordination: payment → restaurant → driver → delivery)"]},{"name":"Delivery & Logistics","classification":"core","aggregates":["Driver (root: DriverId. VOs: Location, VehicleInfo, AvailabilityStatus. Invariants: one active delivery at a time, must be online to receive dispatch)","DeliveryAssignment (root: AssignmentId. Links OrderId ↔ DriverId. VOs: PickupLocation, DropoffLocation, ETA. Invariants: cannot reassign after pickup)"]},{"name":"Restaurant Catalog & Operations","classification":"core","aggregates":["Restaurant (root: RestaurantId. Entities: MenuItem, MenuCategory. VOs: OperatingHours, PrepTimeEstimate, Address. Invariants: menu items must belong to a category, restaurant must be online to receive orders)","IncomingOrder (root: IncomingOrderId. Mirrors order from Order Lifecycle with restaurant-specific view. Invariants: must accept/reject within timeout)"]},{"name":"Customer Experience","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CustomerProfile (root: CustomerId. VOs: SavedAddress, PaymentMethodRef, Favorite. Invariants: at least one address for ordering)"]},{"name":"Payments & Billing","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["PaymentIntent (root: PaymentIntentId. VOs: Amount, Currency, Status. Invariants: capture ≤ authorized amount, refund ≤ captured amount)","Payout (root: PayoutId. VOs: PayoutPeriod, Amount, RecipientRef. Invariants: payout only for completed deliveries)","PromoCode (root: PromoCodeId. VOs: Discount, ValidityPeriod, UsageLimit. Invariants: cannot exceed usage limit, must be within validity period)"]},{"name":"Identity & Access","classification":"generic","aggregates":["User (root: UserId. VOs: Email, PhoneNumber, Role. Standard auth concerns — prefer off-the-shelf)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationPreference (root: UserId. VOs: Channel, Frequency. No complex invariants — template-driven)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Lifecycle","downstream":"Customer Experience","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Lifecycle publishes order status; Customer Experience conforms to its events for tracking UI."},{"upstream":"Order Lifecycle","downstream":"Restaurant Catalog & Operations","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Lifecycle sends orders; Restaurant context can accept/reject but conforms to Order's published language for order structure."},{"upstream":"Order Lifecycle","downstream":"Delivery & Logistics","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Lifecycle publishes dispatch-ready events; Delivery context conforms."},{"upstream":"Order Lifecycle","downstream":"Payments & Billing","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Lifecycle triggers payment auth/capture/refund; Payments conforms to order events."},{"upstream":"Payments & Billing","downstream":"External Payment Gateway","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer — Stripe/Adyen models translated at the boundary. No external concepts leak into domain."},{"upstream":"Restaurant Catalog & Operations","downstream":"Customer Experience","relationship":"Open Host Service — Restaurant context exposes menu/availability as a published language consumed by Customer read projections."},{"upstream":"All Domain Contexts","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist — Notifications subscribes to all domain events and conforms entirely. No upstream influence."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Event-driven communication between contexts instead of synchronous RPC","gains":"Low coupling, independent deployability, natural audit trail, resilience to downstream failures, enables real-time updates","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency (customer might briefly see stale order status), operational complexity of event infrastructure, harder to debug distributed flows","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous REST between services — simpler but creates tight coupling and cascading failures","Shared database — simplest but destroys context boundaries and creates deployment coupling"]},{"decision":"Start as modular monolith, not microservices","gains":"Faster initial development, simpler deployment and debugging, no distributed systems tax on day one, refactoring across boundaries is cheap","sacrifices":"Cannot independently scale contexts initially, risk of boundary erosion if discipline lapses","alternatives_considered":["Microservices from day one — premature for a new platform, high operational overhead before product-market fit"]},{"decision":"Order Lifecycle as orchestrating saga rather than pure choreography","gains":"Centralized view of order state, easier to reason about failure cases, clear cancellation/compensation logic, simpler debugging","sacrifices":"Order Lifecycle becomes a coordination bottleneck, slightly higher coupling than pure choreography","alternatives_considered":["Pure choreography with no orchestrator — elegant but makes it very hard to answer 'what state is this order in?' and handle compensation"]},{"decision":"CQRS for restaurant browsing and order tracking","gains":"Read path optimized independently (search indexes, denormalized views), write path focused on correctness, scales reads independently","sacrifices":"Additional infrastructure (read model projections, eventual consistency between write and read sides)","alternatives_considered":["Single model for reads and writes — simpler but menu browsing and order tracking have very different access patterns and scale requirements"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Restaurant integration","evolution_path":"Start with platform-managed menus. Later add POS integration (Square, Toast) behind an ACL. The Restaurant Catalog boundary already isolates this."},{"area":"Delivery dispatch algorithm","evolution_path":"Start with simple nearest-available-driver. Evolve to ML-based matching considering driver preferences, traffic, batching. Algorithm is encapsulated inside Delivery & Logistics context."},{"area":"Payment providers","evolution_path":"ACL around payment gateway means swapping Stripe for Adyen (or adding both) is contained. No domain model changes needed."},{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"Add new channels (WhatsApp, in-app chat) by extending the generic Notifications context. No impact on core domain."},{"area":"Service extraction","evolution_path":"Each bounded context has explicit event-based interfaces. Extract any context to its own service when scaling demands it — the modular monolith boundaries make this mechanical."},{"area":"Multi-tenant / white-label","evolution_path":"Restaurant Catalog and Customer Experience can be extended with tenant/brand dimensions without touching Order Lifecycle or Delivery."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order state machine","reason":"The order state transitions (Placed → Accepted → Preparing → ReadyForPickup → PickedUp → Delivered, with Cancel/Refund branches) are the core invariant of the system. Changes here cascade everywhere. This rigidity is intentional — order states are the shared language across all actors."},{"area":"Payment authorization before order confirmation","reason":"Payment must be authorized before the order reaches the restaurant. This ordering is non-negotiable for financial integrity. The saga enforces this sequence."},{"area":"Domain event contracts","reason":"Once published, event schemas become contracts. Breaking changes require versioning. This is necessary rigidity — events are the integration backbone."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Real-time location tracking at scale","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use a dedicated real-time infrastructure (Redis Streams or dedicated service like Google Maps Platform) for driver location. Keep it outside the main event bus — location updates are high-frequency, low-durability data. Don't put GPS pings through the same infrastructure as business events."},{"risk":"Order state consistency under concurrent updates","severity":"high","mitigation":"Order aggregate enforces state transitions with optimistic concurrency. Saga pattern handles cross-context coordination with compensating actions. Idempotency keys on all commands."},{"risk":"Driver dispatch fairness and reliability","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Dispatch assignment with timeout + fallback. If a driver doesn't accept within N seconds, reassign. Track acceptance rates. Dead-letter queue for unmatched orders with alerting."},{"risk":"Restaurant goes offline mid-order","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Order saga handles restaurant rejection/timeout as a compensating action: refund payment authorization, notify customer, suggest alternatives. Heartbeat mechanism for restaurant online status."},{"risk":"Event ordering and duplicate delivery","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Idempotent event handlers. Ordering guaranteed within a partition (partition by OrderId). Exactly-once semantics not required if handlers are idempotent."},{"risk":"Modular monolith boundary erosion","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Enforce context boundaries via module visibility rules (e.g., ArchUnit tests, package-private classes, or separate Gradle modules). Code review discipline. No direct database access across context boundaries."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Build as a modular monolith with clear module boundaries per bounded context. Use an in-process event bus (e.g., Spring ApplicationEvents, MediatR, or a simple pub/sub) for inter-context communication. Single database with schema-per-context isolation. Phase 2: Add CQRS read models for restaurant search (Elasticsearch) and order tracking (denormalized view). Phase 3: Extract Delivery & Logistics to its own service first (it has the most distinct scaling and real-time requirements). Phase 4: Extract remaining contexts as needed based on scaling data.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/order-lifecycle/ — Order aggregate, OrderSaga, state machine, domain events","src/restaurant/ — Restaurant aggregate, Menu entities, IncomingOrder, ACL for POS integrations","src/delivery/ — Driver aggregate, DispatchService, LocationTracker, matching algorithm","src/customer/ — CustomerProfile, read projections for browsing and tracking","src/payments/ — PaymentIntent, PayoutService, ACL for payment gateway","src/notifications/ — Event subscribers, template engine, channel dispatchers","src/identity/ — Thin wrapper around auth provider (Auth0/Cognito)","src/shared-kernel/ — Common value objects (Money, Address, GeoLocation), event base types","infrastructure/event-bus/ — In-process event bus abstraction (replaceable with Kafka/SNS later)","infrastructure/persistence/ — Repository implementations, schema-per-context migrations"],"patterns_to_follow":"Hexagonal architecture within each context: domain core has no infrastructure dependencies. Ports (interfaces) defined in domain, adapters in infrastructure. All inter-context communication through domain events — no direct method calls across context boundaries. Aggregate roots are the only entry points for commands. Value objects for everything without identity.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Sharing database tables across contexts — leads to invisible coupling and deployment lock-step","Putting business logic in event handlers — events should trigger commands, not contain decision logic","Over-engineering the dispatch algorithm before having real driver data — start simple, measure, then optimize","Treating driver location as a domain event — it's infrastructure data, not a business event. Use a separate real-time channel","Building microservices before finding product-market fit — the distributed systems tax will slow you down when speed matters most","Synchronous calls between contexts disguised as 'just a quick API call' — this always grows into tight coupling"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Bounded context boundaries — restaurant, order, delivery, payments are natural seams with minimal overlap","Event-driven inter-context communication — well-established pattern for this domain","Order lifecycle as orchestrating saga — industry-proven approach","Modular monolith first — correct for a new platform without proven scale requirements"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact driver dispatch algorithm and matching strategy — depends heavily on geographic density and driver supply, needs real data","Real-time tracking infrastructure choice — depends on expected driver count and update frequency, needs load testing","Payout calculation complexity — restaurant commission models, driver incentives, and tax implications vary wildly by market"],"assumptions":["Single geographic market initially — multi-market adds significant complexity to delivery logistics and payments","Mobile-first customers and drivers, web for restaurant dashboard","Standard payment flows (card-based) — no cash on delivery initially","English-only initially — i18n is a later concern","No grocery/convenience — restaurant food delivery only"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"A food delivery platform connecting restaurants, customers, and delivery drivers — designed as a modular system with six bounded contexts. The architecture uses event-driven communication between contexts to keep coupling low, a central Order Lifecycle context as the orchestrating core domain, and clear anti-corruption layers between the three actor types (restaurant, customer, driver). The design prioritizes independent evolvability of each context, real-time location/stat
